content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF page 204 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_001_page_001.jpg

1. Paul and Silvanus and Timothy, to the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ:

2. Grace to you and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

3. We are bound to give thanks to God always for you, brothers, as is fitting, because your faith grows exceedingly, and the love of each one of you all toward one another abounds,

4. so that we ourselves boast of you in the churches of God for your steadfastness and faith in all your persecutions and the afflictions that you are enduring —

5. a clear evidence of the righteous judgment of God, that you may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you also suffer;

6. since indeed it is righteous before God to repay with affliction those who afflict you,

7. and to you who are afflicted, rest with us, at the revelation of the Lord Jesus from heaven with the angels of his power,

8. in flaming fire, giving vengeance to those who do not know God and to those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus,

9. who shall suffer the penalty of eternal destruction away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his might,

10. when he comes to be glorified in his saints and to be marveled at among all who believed — because our testimony to you was believed — in that day.

11. To this end we also pray always for you, that our God may count you worthy of the calling, and may fulfill every desire of goodness and every work of faith with power,

12. so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **The Prescript (vv.1-2):** The opening follows the same tripartite authorship as 1 Thessalonians — Paul, Silvanus (Silas), and Timothy. Sinaiticus reads "God our Father" (θεω πατρι ημων) in v.1, whereas some later manuscripts read "God the Father." The greeting in v.2 is formulaic Pauline: grace (χαρις) and peace (ειρηνη), combining Greek and Hebrew salutatory conventions.

2. **"We are bound" (v.3):** οφειλομεν — a word of obligation, not mere voluntariness. Paul frames thanksgiving not as optional sentiment but as a duty owed to God. This is a distinctive Pauline expression found only in the Thessalonian correspondence (cf. 2 Thes 2:13). The language suggests some in Thessalonica may have questioned whether their suffering disqualified them from praise.

3. **"Clear evidence of the righteous judgment" (v.5):** ενδειγμα της δικαιας κρισεως — their endurance under persecution is itself the proof (ενδειγμα, a hapax legomenon in the NT) that God's coming judgment is just. The logic: if they suffer for the kingdom now, God's future vindication must follow, or God would be unrighteous.

4. **Retributive Justice (vv.6-9):** The passage employs strict lex talionis vocabulary: "repay" (ανταποδουναι) affliction to the afflicters, rest (ανεσιν) to the afflicted. The "flaming fire" (εν πυρι φλογος) in v.8 follows the Sinaiticus reading; some manuscripts reverse to "flame of fire" (φλογι πυρος). The phrase echoes Isaiah 66:15 and Exodus 3:2, linking eschatological judgment to theophanic tradition.

5. **"Eternal destruction" (v.9):** ολεθρον αιωνιον — not annihilation but ruinous separation. The preposition απο ("away from") defines the destruction as exclusion from the Lord's presence and glory, rather than cessation of existence. This parallels Isaiah 2:10, 19, 21 (LXX), where humans flee "from the face of the terror of the Lord."

6. **The Parenthetical in v.10:** The clause "because our testimony to you was believed" (οτι επιστευθη το μαρτυριον ημων εφ υμας) breaks the syntactic flow. It is a pastoral aside — Paul reminds the Thessalonians that they are among those who believed, and therefore among those in whom Christ will be glorified. The shift from aorist (επιστευθη, "was believed") to future (ελθη, "when he comes") compresses past conversion and future consummation into a single theological horizon.

7. **"Every desire of goodness" (v.11):** πασαν ευδοκιαν αγαθωσυνης — the genitive can be subjective ("every good desire") or objective ("every desire for goodness"). In formal equivalence, we preserve the ambiguity. The prayer is that God would both inspire and complete the Thessalonians' moral striving — goodness is not self-generated but divinely empowered.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
